questions.question: What physical process underlies SPDC entangled photon generation?

options.text: A pump photon splits into two lower-energy photons in a nonlinear crystal
Two photons combine to form one higher-energy photon
Photons are absorbed and re-emitted by atoms
Photons tunnel through a potential barrier together

questions.explanation: SPDC (Spontaneous Parametric Down-Conversion) occurs when a pump photon interacts with a χ⁽²⁾ nonlinear crystal and spontaneously converts into two lower-energy photons (signal and idler) that are correlated in energy, momentum, and polarization.

questions.question: What distinguishes Type-I from Type-II SPDC?

options.text: Type-I uses visible light, Type-II uses infrared
Type-I produces photons with same polarization, Type-II produces orthogonal
Type-I is faster than Type-II
Type-I uses one crystal, Type-II uses two

questions.explanation: In Type-I SPDC, signal and idler photons have the same polarization (e.g., both horizontal). In Type-II SPDC, they have orthogonal polarizations (one horizontal, one vertical), which directly creates polarization entanglement from spatial overlap.

questions.question: How does fine structure splitting affect quantum dot entanglement?

options.text: It increases the pair generation rate
It provides which-path information that degrades entanglement
It has no effect on entanglement quality
It improves the wavelength purity

questions.explanation: Fine structure splitting creates an energy difference between the two exciton states, causing a measurable time difference in the cascade paths. This provides which-path information that reduces entanglement fidelity. Ideally, splitting should be zero or much smaller than ℏ/τ.

questions.question: In the DLCZ protocol, how is entanglement heralded?

options.text: By measuring the atomic states directly
By detecting a single photon after beam splitter interference
By measuring both photons in coincidence
By applying a verification pulse

questions.explanation: DLCZ uses heralding: photons from two ensembles interfere at a beam splitter, erasing which-path information. Detection of exactly one photon (not zero, not two) heralds that the ensembles are entangled without revealing which one contains the excitation.

questions.question: What does concurrence measure for a two-qubit state?

options.text: The purity of the quantum state
The degree of entanglement (0 = separable, 1 = maximally entangled)
The fidelity to a Bell state
The violation of Bell inequalities

questions.explanation: Concurrence is an entanglement monotone that ranges from 0 (separable states) to 1 (maximally entangled states like Bell states). It quantifies the amount of entanglement present and is monotonically related to entanglement of formation.

questions.question: Why is SPDC considered probabilistic while quantum dots can be deterministic?

options.text: SPDC requires quantum measurement but quantum dots do not
SPDC conversion probability is very low; QDs can be triggered on demand
SPDC uses classical light sources
Quantum dots are always in an excited state

questions.explanation: SPDC has very low conversion efficiency (~10⁻¹² per pump photon), making pair generation probabilistic. Quantum dots can be optically or electrically triggered to reliably emit one pair per excitation, approaching deterministic operation when combined with cavities.

questions.question: A Bell parameter S > 2 proves the presence of quantum entanglement.
questions.explanation: True. The CHSH inequality states that any local hidden variable theory gives S ≤ 2. Observing S > 2 (up to 2√2 ≈ 2.83 for maximally entangled states) proves quantum correlations that cannot be explained classically, confirming genuine entanglement.
